name: boltdb/bolt
why: Single-file embedded KV with a B+tree on mmap, small enough to hold in your head — the cleanest MVCC-via-COW I've read in open source. Tag v1.3.1 is the last release before archive.

entry_points.label: Trace a Put through a write transaction
entry_points.question: Where does the copy-on-write actually happen, and how do in-flight readers see a consistent snapshot of the old tree while a writer is mutating pages?

entry_points.label: Understand the freelist
entry_points.question: How does Bolt reclaim freed pages without breaking readers that still hold pointers into the old MVCC snapshot?

name: golang/groupcache
why: Brad Fitzpatrick's distributed in-process cache. The singleflight trick — deduping concurrent fetches — is a small file with a big lesson.

entry_points.label: Read singleflight
entry_points.question: When N goroutines all ask for the same key at the same time, how does singleflight ensure only one actually fetches it, and what guarantees do the other N-1 callers get about the value they receive (and any error)?

entry_points.label: Read the consistent hash ring
entry_points.question: How does groupcache decide which peer owns a key, and what happens to the distribution when a peer is added or removed?

name: antirez/redis (1.0.0)
why: Antirez's first stable Redis — the whole server, every command, and the event loop in roughly ten thousand lines of C, pinned to the 1.0.0 release commit (no v1.0.0 tag was ever pushed to GitHub). Read it to see what the server actually does when a SET arrives, before later refactors split redis.c into a dozen files.

entry_points.label: Trace a SET from socket to dict
entry_points.question: When a client sends `SET key value`, what's the path from aeMain's select-wakeup, through command parsing and dispatch in redis.c, to the dictAdd that stores the entry — and how does the single-threaded server interleave this request with others on the same iteration of the event loop?

name: sqlite/sqlite (btree, version-3.53.1)
why: The B-tree that backs every table and index in SQLite, pinned to 3.53.1 — about ten thousand lines inside the larger amalgamation. Read it for how on-disk b-tree pages, cursors, and balancing actually work in production code.

entry_points.label: Insert a row through the b-tree
entry_points.question: When sqlite3BtreeInsert is called for a new (key, payload) on a table b-tree, what's the path through btree.c — moveto via the cursor, allocating space on the target leaf page, splitting and rebalancing siblings when the leaf is full, and updating parent pointers — until the cell is written into a page buffer the pager will later flush?

name: git/git (v0.99)
why: Git when it was still a 'stupid content tracker' — plumbing only, before porcelain commands existed as anything but shell scripts. Read the object database, the index, and the earliest sketch of merge with no high-level glue in the way.

entry_points.label: Read the object store
entry_points.question: When a file is hashed into the object database at v0.99, what exactly does sha1_file.c write under `.git/objects/`, and how does cat-file.c locate that object on disk — what's the on-disk shape of a blob/tree/commit before packfiles existed?

entry_points.label: Read the index
entry_points.question: How is the index file laid out at v0.99, what does update-cache do when staging a path, and at what step inside write-tree does the in-memory index actually become a tree object on disk?

name: BurntSushi/ripgrep
why: Andrew Gallant's grep replacement and a clinic in "fast for real users" — every interesting decision in tool building made out loud, from the regex engine choice to mmap heuristics to gitignore-aware filesystem walking.

entry_points.label: Architecture tour: a single grep through searcher and matcher
entry_points.question: What's the shape of a single grep run through ripgrep — main → walk → searcher → matcher → printer — and which boundary is where "this is fast" actually gets earned (literal-prefix detection, mmap vs streaming, Sink design)?

entry_points.label: Read the ignore crate
entry_points.question: How does ripgrep walk a directory tree while respecting .gitignore at every depth, and what's the relationship between the global, parent, and per-directory ignore patterns?

name: junegunn/fzf
why: The matching engine and scoring that make interactive fuzzy search feel instant on a million-line input. Surprisingly small.

entry_points.label: Read the matching algorithm
entry_points.question: What does FuzzyMatchV2 do differently from V1, and how do the scoring components (gap penalty, consecutive-match bonus, case-sensitivity bonus) compose into a single int?

name: kubernetes/kubernetes · pkg/scheduler
why: Kubernetes' decision-making for 'which pod goes on which node' — a real distributed scheduler with pluggable policies, living in `pkg/scheduler/` of the main kubernetes/kubernetes repo. Read it to see how predicate-and-priority became filter-and-score, and how a queue handles backoff, preemption, and unschedulable retries.

entry_points.label: Trace a pod from queue to bound
entry_points.question: When a new Pod hits the scheduler, what's the path from the scheduling queue through filter plugins, score plugins, and final binding — and where exactly does the algorithm decide a node is infeasible vs just lower-scoring?

name: etcd-io/raft
why: The standalone Raft implementation extracted from etcd. The whole protocol — leader election, log replication, snapshot, joint consensus — is a few thousand lines of Go you can hold in your head. The reference implementation many other systems consult.

entry_points.label: Read leader election
entry_points.question: When a node times out without hearing from the leader, what's the exact sequence of state transitions, RPCs sent and received, and tie-breaking until exactly one node becomes leader (or another timeout fires)?

entry_points.label: Read log replication and storage
entry_points.question: How does the leader commit a log entry — what does 'majority replicated' mean in this code, when does an entry become 'applied' vs just 'committed', and what's the contract with the storage interface?

name: fluent/fluent-bit
why: Log/metrics shipper at the heart of a million Kubernetes clusters. Event loop, pluggable input/filter/output chain, and a real budget for memory and CPU — read it to see what 'lightweight' actually buys in C.

entry_points.label: Trace a record through the pipeline
entry_points.question: From an input plugin emitting a record to an output plugin sending it elsewhere, what's the in-process path — when does the record become a chunk, where does routing happen, and what owns the memory at each hop?

name: nginx/nginx
why: The textbook C event-driven HTTP server — workers, the connection pool, and an eleven-phase request pipeline. Read it for the lifecycle of one HTTP request through the phase engine, not the configuration parser or the broader module system.

entry_points.label: Trace an HTTP request through the phase engine
entry_points.question: From the moment ngx_event_accept hands a new TCP connection to ngx_http_init_connection, what's the path through the eleven request-processing phases (declared in ngx_http_core_module.c) until a handler in NGX_HTTP_CONTENT_PHASE produces a response?

name: lua/lua (v5.4.7)
why: The Lua 5.4 reference implementation — roughly seventeen thousand lines of meticulously small C. The interpreter dispatch in lvm.c is the cleanest 'bytecode VM' you can read, free of JIT machinery.

entry_points.label: Read the bytecode interpreter
entry_points.question: In lvm.c's luaV_execute, how does the dispatch loop fetch and decode the next instruction from a CallInfo's saved program counter, and when OP_CALL runs, what determines whether the call descends into a fresh luaV_execute frame, tail-calls in place, or jumps out to a C function — and where does the matching OP_RETURN logic re-enter the outer luaV_execute?

name: simonw/sqlite-utils
why: Pythonic wrapper over sqlite3 — read it to see what minimum API turns the stdlib module from awkward into pleasant.

entry_points.label: Read db.py top to bottom
entry_points.question: What's the minimum API surface that makes the Python sqlite3 module pleasant to use, and how does sqlite-utils handle the impedance between Python dicts and SQL columns (types, NULLs, primary keys, foreign keys)?

name: alicebob/miniredis
why: A test-time fake Redis written in Go — small enough to read in a sitting, big enough to teach you what the Redis wire protocol actually looks like.

entry_points.label: Trace a SET command from socket to reply
entry_points.question: How does miniredis read a RESP command off the socket, dispatch it, and write a RESP reply — and where exactly does the in-memory datastore get touched in that round-trip?

name: tinygrad/tinygrad (v0.4.0)
why: tinygrad pinned to v0.4.0, when the whole framework — tensors, autograd, math ops, lazy graph, CPU/GPU backends — fit in roughly three thousand lines of Python. The 'PyTorch but readable' pitch is still true at this tag; later versions add a kernel compiler and grow substantially.

entry_points.label: Trace an op forward and backward
entry_points.question: At v0.4.0, when you call `(a * b).sum().backward()` on two tinygrad Tensors, what's the path from tensor.py's __mul__ through an mlops.py Function (forward + backward) into a kernel in llops/ops_cpu.py — and when backward() runs, how does the autograd graph recorded in tensor.py walk back through Function instances to call each .backward() in the right order?

name: ggerganov/llama.cpp (sampling, b9174)
why: Pinned to release b9174 and scoped to the token-sampling chain — the part that actually picks the next token after the model produces logits. Read it to internalize how top-k, top-p, temperature, and repetition-penalty compose into a real sampling pipeline, and where randomness enters.

entry_points.label: Read the sampler chain
entry_points.question: When the per-request sampler chain in common/sampling.cpp processes a fresh set of logits, in what order do the top-k, top-p, temperature, and repetition-penalty samplers in src/llama-sampler.cpp transform the candidate distribution — and where inside llama_sampler_sample does the final random draw actually happen, with what RNG state?

name: ggerganov/whisper.cpp
why: A real transformer (the original Whisper) running end-to-end in plain C++ with no Python, no PyTorch, no ONNX runtime — just ggml tensors and a single source file. The clearest demonstration that "ML inference" is mostly arithmetic.

entry_points.label: Trace audio through to text
entry_points.question: From a 16kHz WAV input to a text transcript: where does the mel-spectrogram get computed, what tensors does the encoder produce, and how does the decoder loop choose tokens?

entry_points.label: Understand model loading and quantization
entry_points.question: When whisper-cpp loads a quantized model file (q5_0, q8_0, etc.), how is the weight data laid out in memory and how do the matrix-multiply kernels in ggml.c consume it without a full dequantization step?

name: openzfs/zfs (ARC, zfs-2.4.2)
why: OpenZFS's Adaptive Replacement Cache — Megiddo and Modha's algorithm with ZFS-specific extensions for ghost lists, MFU/MRU rebalancing, and compressed buffers, pinned to zfs-2.4.2. Scoped to the read path so the famous 'adaptive boundary' is visible in code without taking on writeback or compression internals.

entry_points.label: Read the ARC adaptive read path
entry_points.question: When arc_read is called for a block buffer that hits in either the MFU or MRU list, what's the path that returns it to the caller — and on a miss, how does the ghost-list bookkeeping in arc.c decide whether to grow the MFU or MRU target size (the adaptive boundary `arc_p`) before initiating the underlying I/O?

name: python/cpython (dictobject.c, v3.13.0)
why: The CPython dict at 3.13 — the compact-and-ordered hash table that backs everything from globals() to keyword arguments. Pinned to a settled release so the compact representation and insertion-order guarantee are stable; scoped to insertion and probing, not the full rehash/resize story.

entry_points.label: Trace an insertion through the open-addressing probe
entry_points.question: When `d[key] = value` reaches insertdict() in Objects/dictobject.c, how does the probing sequence (the `5*i + 1 + perturb` scheme) walk the compact `ix` array described in PyDictKeysObject to find either an existing slot or a free one — and where exactly does insertion order get recorded so that iterating the dict later produces keys in the order they were first added?

reading.title: dictnotes.txt — design notes on CPython's compact dict

name: v8/v8 (Ignition, 15.0.99)
why: V8's bytecode interpreter, pinned to 15.0.99 — the layer that runs JavaScript before TurboFan compiles it, dispatching one opcode at a time. The smallest complete view of 'interpreter inside a JIT.'

entry_points.label: Read the bytecode dispatch loop
entry_points.question: How does Ignition's bytecode interpreter (built by interpreter-generator.cc using CSA) dispatch from one bytecode handler to the next at runtime, and where does the bytecode array's offset get updated to point at the next instruction?

name: llvm/llvm-project (mem2reg)
why: LLVM's 'promote alloca to SSA register' pass — the optimization that turns memory-shaped IR into the SSA form every later pass operates on. The smallest LLVM transform that uses the dominator tree non-trivially.

entry_points.label: Read the promotion pass
entry_points.question: When mem2reg promotes a stack alloca to SSA registers, where does it insert the phi nodes and how does it use the dominator tree (via ForwardIDFCalculator) to know which load reads from which store?

name: postgres/postgres (executor SeqScan, REL_18_4)
why: Postgres pinned to 18.4, scoped to one trace: how `SELECT * FROM t WHERE x > 5` actually executes, from ExecutorStart through one tuple coming out of a SeqScan. The cleanest entry into the Volcano iterator framework that powers every Postgres query.

entry_points.label: Walk one tuple from ExecutorStart through SeqScan
entry_points.question: For `SELECT * FROM t WHERE x > 5`, starting at execMain.c's ExecutorStart and following ExecProcNode through one iteration of the SeqScanState produced by ExecInitSeqScan, where is the heap tuple actually fetched, where is the `x > 5` qual evaluated, and what does the SeqScan node return to its parent on each call — both the steady-state case and when the heap is exhausted?

name: duckdb/duckdb (planner)
why: A modern analytical SQL planner small enough to finish reading. The Binder → LogicalPlan → Optimizer pipeline is well-named and easy to trace — read it to see how a textbook query plan actually looks in code.

entry_points.label: Trace a SELECT from parser to plan
entry_points.question: From `SELECT a, sum(b) FROM t WHERE c > 5 GROUP BY a`, what does Binder produce for the column references and the aggregate, and where in the LogicalPlan tree does the WHERE clause end up — above or below the aggregation?

name: rui314/chibicc
why: Rui Ueyama's small C compiler — compiles a real subset of C to x86-64 assembly, with each phase isolated in its own file. The cleanest hand-written compiler available to read.

entry_points.label: Trace a compilation top to bottom
entry_points.question: When chibicc compiles `int main() { return 1+2; }`, what does the token stream look like, what AST does parse.c build, and which x86-64 instructions does codegen.c emit for the addition and the return?

name: munificent/craftinginterpreters (tree-walker)
why: jlox is a complete language interpreter written across one book — the cleanest tree-walking interpreter I know, and the resolver pass teaches a lesson you don't get from textbooks.

entry_points.label: Trace an expression from source to value
entry_points.question: How does an expression like `(1 + 2) * 3` travel from source characters to a runtime double — what's the shape of the AST nodes (see Expr.java) in between, and where do precedence and associativity get encoded in Parser.java?

entry_points.label: Read the resolver
entry_points.question: Why does jlox need a separate variable-resolution pass — what bug does it fix, and how does the pass map a name to a static scope distance?

name: benbjohnson/litestream (v0.5.11)
why: Streaming replication for SQLite — a Go daemon that watches a SQLite WAL and ships frames to S3-compatible storage continuously. Pinned to v0.5.11; read it for how 'replication as a sidecar' is structured around the WAL file format that SQLite was already going to write anyway.

entry_points.label: Trace a WAL frame from sync to replica
entry_points.question: When litestream's monitor sees the SQLite WAL grow because a new frame has landed, what's the path in db.go that opens the WAL, parses the new frame's header (page number, checksum, commit marker), copies the page bytes — and what does replica.go then do to ship that frame to the configured replica client before the next WAL checkpoint potentially overwrites it?

name: mobile-shell/mosh
why: Mobile shell — replaces SSH for high-latency or roaming connections. The State Synchronization Protocol (SSP) it invented is the lesson: send "what the terminal should look like", not "what to type".

entry_points.label: Read the network layer
entry_points.question: How does mosh frame, sequence, and (re)transmit datagrams over a single UDP socket, and how does it survive a NAT rebinding or IP change without resetting the session?

entry_points.label: Read the state synchronization
entry_points.question: What is mosh actually sending across the wire — diffs of terminal state, or full snapshots, or both — and how does it decide?

name: tigerbeetle/tigerbeetle (vsr.zig)
why: TigerBeetle's Viewstamped Replication Revisited in Zig — a clean modern consensus implementation you can read top to bottom. Read it to see VSR (the alternative to Raft) the way the Liskov paper actually describes it, written to be deterministic and testable.

entry_points.label: Read the replica state machine
entry_points.question: When a primary fails and a view change starts, how does replica.zig decide who the new primary is, how does it reconcile its journal with peers during the do-view-change step, and at what point does the new primary start accepting fresh client requests again?

name: tailscale/tailscale (tsnet)
why: Userspace WireGuard wrapped as a Go library — embed it and your program becomes its own node on a tailnet, no daemon needed. The tsnet package is the small API surface that hides wgengine and magicsock; read it for what 'tailscale-in-a-binary' actually looks like, not for the lower layers underneath.

entry_points.label: Read the tsnet API surface
entry_points.question: When a Go program calls `(&tsnet.Server{}).Listen("tcp", ":80")` and starts serving, what does tsnet.go do on first Up — how does it materialize a node identity, bring up the wgengine/magicsock stack it depends on, and hand a real net.Listener back to the caller — and at what point does the returned listener start accepting connections from peer nodes on the tailnet?

name: FiloSottile/age
why: A small, modern file-encryption tool whose entire format spec fits on one page. Read it to see how existing primitives (X25519, ChaCha20-Poly1305, HKDF) compose into a complete encrypted file format.

entry_points.label: Trace an X25519 encryption
entry_points.question: When `age -r <recipient> <file>` runs, how does the recipient's X25519 public key become a wrapped file key, and where in the code does the wrapped key get serialized into the file header?

name: busybox/busybox (coreutils/cat.c)
why: Every busybox applet is shaped the same way; cat.c is the smallest complete example of that shape.

entry_points.label: Read cat.c in full and identify the applet contract
entry_points.question: What does busybox demand of every applet (name, argv parsing, return code, output handling), and how does cat.c satisfy each of those in under 200 lines?

name: busybox/busybox (coreutils/wc.c)
why: Three counters in a single pass over arbitrary input — the shape of the loop is the lesson.

entry_points.label: Read wc.c and identify the multi-counter pattern
entry_points.question: Where in the main loop does wc commit to counting one of bytes / characters / words / lines, and how does the multibyte-input handling thread through that decision?

name: gnu/grep
why: The reference GNU grep. Read it to internalize how a real production regex tool composes DFA construction, Boyer-Moore literal scanning, and line buffering into a search loop that stays fast on adversarial inputs.

entry_points.label: Read the search loop
entry_points.question: How does GNU grep handle a regex pattern against a giant input stream — where does the DFA construction happen, and what tricks (Boyer-Moore, line buffering, byte-aware scans) does it use to stay fast on the common case?

name: Hobbit netcat (nc110)
why: Hobbit's original netcat, 1995 — a swiss-army-knife for TCP/UDP in under 1000 lines of K&R-flavored C. Predates 'nc' being a verb. The smallest complete network tool worth reading.

entry_points.label: Read main() and identify what netcat actually does
entry_points.question: How few lines of C does it take to open a TCP socket (connect or listen), copy stdin↔socket bidirectionally, and exit cleanly when either side closes? Then: what does netcat layer on top of that core to become useful?

name: openbsd/src (sbin/ping/ping.c)
why: BSD ping in one .c file — the textbook implementation of ICMP echo, RTT timing, and raw sockets, kept readable by OpenBSD's house style. Read it to see what 'just send a packet and wait' looks like when there's no library to hide behind.

entry_points.label: Read the ICMP echo loop
entry_points.question: How does ping.c build an ICMP echo request with sequence number and timestamp, get it onto a raw socket, and match each reply back to its send time — and what does it actually do with kernel-provided timestamps vs. its own gettimeofday for the round-trip measurement?

name: curl/curl (urlapi, curl-8_20_0)
why: The URL parser of curl, pinned to 8.20.0 — a self-contained study in 'what does a URL actually mean.' Covers scheme, userinfo, host, port, path, query, and fragment, plus relative-resolution, IDN, and percent-encoding.

entry_points.label: Parse a URL end to end
entry_points.question: When you call `curl_url_set(u, CURLUPART_URL, "https://user:pass@host:8443/path?q=1#frag", 0)`, what's the path through lib/urlapi.c that splits the input into the seven URL parts — and how does the parser distinguish a relative reference like `page.html?x=1` (which must be resolved against a base) from an absolute URL it can split on its own?

name: mit-pdos/xv6-riscv
why: MIT's teaching xv6 ported to RISC-V — the entire Unix-like kernel in roughly six thousand lines, designed to be read end-to-end. The entry point picks the boot path so the first session has a defined finish line; later reads can follow the file system or one syscall.

entry_points.label: Walk the boot path to the first scheduler tick
entry_points.question: From the very first instruction in kernel/entry.S after the SBI hands control over, what's the exact sequence — switch to S-mode in start.c, jump into main.c's main(), bring up paging/traps/the scheduler, fork the first user process — until proc.c's scheduler() loop calls swtch() (from kernel/swtch.S) to load that user process's context for the first time?

name: mirror/busybox (init.c, 1_36_1)
why: BusyBox's init — a complete System V style PID 1 in one C file, the kind of init that boots most embedded Linux. Pinned to 1.36.1; read it for the actual reaper-and-respawn loop, parsing /etc/inittab, and what 'PID 1's job' looks like when it isn't systemd.

entry_points.label: Read the init lifecycle
entry_points.question: When BusyBox init starts as PID 1, what's the structure of the main loop after the boot-time actions have run — how does it simultaneously wait for SIGCHLD from respawned children, dispatch the respawn logic for each, and reap orphaned processes that the kernel reparents to PID 1?

name: openssh/openssh-portable (curve25519 kex, V_10_3_P1)
why: OpenSSH key exchange at 10.3p1, scoped to one method on one side: the client-side curve25519-sha256 handshake. The full kex layer supports a dozen methods plus hybrids (mlkem768x25519, sntrup761x25519); this entry point ignores everything except the modern default so the round trip stays readable in one sitting.

entry_points.label: Trace a curve25519 KEX from the client side
entry_points.question: From the SSH_MSG_KEXINIT message on the client side, what's the path through kex.c that negotiates curve25519-sha256 from the server's algorithm list, then through kexgen.c's kex_gen_client and kexc25519.c's kex_c25519_keygen/kex_c25519_shared_key until the shared secret K is derived — and at exactly which points in that sequence does the client send SSH_MSG_KEX_ECDH_INIT and consume SSH_MSG_KEX_ECDH_REPLY?

name: kraj/musl (vfprintf.c, v1.2.5)
why: musl libc's printf — the entire C printf family funnels through one ~700-line vfprintf.c, pinned to musl 1.2.5. Read it for an unusually clean implementation of the format-specifier mini-language, including the awkward bits like width-from-arg `%*d`, positional `%2$s`, and floating-point conversion.

entry_points.label: Parse a format string and emit one int and one float
entry_points.question: When vfprintf walks a format string like `"%5d %.3f\n"`, how does the inner loop dispatch on each `%` directive — collecting flags, width, precision, length modifier — and then for `%d` pull the int via va_arg and format it, and for `%.3f` pull the double and run it through the floating-point conversion routines defined in vfprintf.c?

name: simonkelley/dnsmasq
why: Small single-binary DNS forwarder + DHCP server running in millions of routers and laptops; the github.com/imp/dnsmasq mirror tracks Simon Kelley's upstream at thekelleys.org.uk. Read it to see how a real network daemon parses RFC 1035 wire-format records, caches answers, and forwards upstream — in a C codebase a determined reader can finish in a weekend.

entry_points.label: Trace a DNS query through forward + cache
entry_points.question: When a client query arrives on the UDP socket, what's the path through forward.c that picks an upstream server, parses the RFC 1035 packet (via rfc1035.c), and decides whether to answer from cache.c or forward — and where does the answer get rewritten with the original transaction ID before being returned to the client?
